original_abstract:
We introduce Graph of Thoughts (GoT), a framework designed to push the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) beyond existing paradigms like Chain-of-Thought or Tree of Thoughts (ToT). The core innovation of GoT lies in its ability to model LLM-generated information as an arbitrary graph. In this structure, individual units of information—or "thoughts"—act as vertices, while edges represent the intricate dependencies between them. 

By move beyond linear or hierarchical structures, GoT enables the model to aggregate disparate ideas into synergistic outcomes, distill knowledge from complex networks of reasoning, and iteratively refine thoughts through feedback loops. Our evaluation demonstrates that GoT significantly outperforms the current state of the art; for instance, it improves sorting quality by 62% over ToT while reducing computational costs by more than 31%. Designed for extensibility, GoT allows for the integration of novel thought transformations, serving as a versatile foundation for future prompting schemes. Ultimately, this framework brings LLM reasoning into closer alignment with the non-linear, recurrent nature of human cognition.
humanized_abstract:
We propose Graph of Thoughts (GoT), a framework that advances the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) significantly compared to state-of-the-art approaches such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) or Tree of Thoughts (ToT). Graph of Thoughts considers the LLM output as a graph in general form. Here, the nodes are the so-called thoughts and the edges represent the relationship between them.

Going beyond the linear or tree-like structure of previous models, GoT enables the model to generate non-trivial compositions of individual input ideas, distill the underlying insights from intricate reasoning, and performs several iterations of postprocessing and refinements via cyclic feedback. Experiments conducted demonstrate that GoT surpasses the existing models, achieving up to 62% higher sorting quality (comparing to 39% for ToT) and more than 31% faster computation speed. In addition, the modularity of GoT allows for easier integration of future thought transformations as well as for supporting diverse forms of future prompting techniques. Therefore, our model is in better alignment with the fundamentally non-linear and recurrent processing characteristics of the human brain.

Going beyond the linear or tree-like structure of previous models, GoT enables the model to generate non-trivial compositions of individual input ideas, distill the underlying insights from intricate reasoning, and performs several iterations of postprocessing and refinements via cyclic feedback. document.input:
We introduce Graph of Thoughts (GoT), a framework designed to push the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) beyond existing paradigms like Chain-of-Thought or Tree of Thoughts (ToT). The core innovation of GoT lies in its ability to model LLM-generated information as an arbitrary graph. In this structure, individual units of information—or "thoughts"—act as vertices, while edges represent the intricate dependencies between them. 

By move beyond linear or hierarchical structures, GoT enables the model to aggregate disparate ideas into synergistic outcomes, distill knowledge from complex networks of reasoning, and iteratively refine thoughts through feedback loops. Our evaluation demonstrates that GoT significantly outperforms the current state of the art; for instance, it improves sorting quality by 62% over ToT while reducing computational costs by more than 31%. Designed for extensibility, GoT allows for the integration of novel thought transformations, serving as a versatile foundation for future prompting schemes. Ultimately, this framework brings LLM reasoning into closer alignment with the non-linear, recurrent nature of human cognition.
